title: Winding for Wave Maps
speaker: Max Engelstein
abstract: Wave maps are harmonic maps from a Lorentzian domain to a Riemannian target. Like solutions to many energy critical PDE, wave maps can develop singularities where the energy concentrates on arbitrary small scales but the norm stays bounded. Zooming in on these singularities yields a harmonic map (called a soliton or bubble) in the weak limit. One fundamental question is whether this weak limit is unique, that is to say, whether different bubbles may appear as the limit of different sequences of rescalings.

proof_stages.label: Energy-critical wave maps and bubbles
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines wave maps as Lorentzian harmonic maps, sets up the Cauchy problem, explains stationary harmonic maps, and shows that in two spatial dimensions the conserved energy is scale-invariant so concentration can produce harmonic-map bubbles.

proof_stages.label: Compactness theory and the soliton-resolution question
proof_stages.summary: Known bubbling examples, the Sterbenz--Tataru dichotomy, noninteracting bubble scales, and the soliton-resolution conjecture are recalled to formulate the uniqueness question for bubbles along continuous time and rescaling parameters.

proof_stages.label: Counterexample theorem near the ground state
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem asserts that a smooth quasi-equivariant wave map into a nonanalytic warped product of a torus and a sphere develops a continuum of distinct bubbles at one spacetime point, so continuous-time soliton resolution fails even in a simple symmetry and near-ground-state energy regime.

proof_stages.label: Parabolic source of the mechanism and nonanalyticity
proof_stages.summary: Topping's harmonic-map heat-flow example, the Lojasiewicz--Simon uniqueness principle for analytic functionals, and smooth flat counterexamples identify nonanalyticity as the ingredient that allows continuum limiting behavior.

proof_stages.label: Hamiltonian obstacles to importing parabolic arguments
proof_stages.summary: ODE analogies show that analytic Hamiltonian dynamics need not converge and that critical sets are not barriers, explaining why the wave-map proof cannot rely on maximum principles or parabolic trapping.

proof_stages.label: Nonanalytic winding target and confined data
proof_stages.summary: The target is built as a twisted product with a torus component containing an infinite winding geodesic gamma and a warping function whose gradient is parallel to gamma, while initial data are chosen in the corresponding totally geodesic gamma-times-sphere submanifold.

proof_stages.label: Winding criterion for nonunique bubbles
proof_stages.summary: A winding singularity is defined by failure of lifted bubbles to have a convergent subsequence in the universal cover, and such winding forces all constant-torus-position ground-state bubbles to appear, contradicting continuous-time soliton resolution.

proof_stages.label: Forcing and classifying the bubble
proof_stages.summary: The proof sketch shows that the alpha component prevents scattering and forces energy concentration, the flow remains in the winding submanifold up to bubbling, and near-ground-state energy forces the bubble image onto the bottom winding circle so that the winding criterion applies.
